_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against AAOS rotator cuff clinical practice guidelines, American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) consensus statements, NICE NG101 shoulder injury guidelines, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Rotator Cuff Tear: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A rotator cuff tear is a disruption of one or more of the four tendons comprising the rotator cuff — the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis — which originate from the scapula and insert around the humeral head to stabilize the shoulder joint during arm movement. Tears may be acute (resulting from a traumatic event such as a fall on an outstretched arm or lifting a heavy object), degenerative (from progressive tendinopathy and microtearing over years of use, particularly in the dominant arm), or a combination of both in which a degenerate tendon tears under even moderate load. The supraspinatus tendon is the most commonly torn, accounting for approximately 85-90% of rotator cuff tears. Prevalence increases significantly with age — studies of asymptomatic individuals using MRI show that approximately 50% of people over 65 have a rotator cuff tear, while symptomatic tears affect 5-10% of the general adult population. Treatment ranges from conservative management with physical therapy in older patients or those with low-demand lifestyles, to arthroscopic or open surgical repair in younger, active patients or those with acute traumatic tears.

keyStats.prevalence: Symptomatic rotator cuff tears affect 5-10% of the general adult population; asymptomatic tears are present in up to 50% of individuals over 65 on MRI
keyStats.demographics: More common in men than women; prevalence increases sharply after age 50; dominant arm affected in 90% of unilateral cases
keyStats.avgAge: Degenerative tears peak in the 60s-70s; acute traumatic tears peak in the 40s-50s (younger patients with higher activity demands)
keyStats.globalCases: Most common cause of shoulder pain and disability in adults over 40; approximately 275,000 rotator cuff surgical repairs performed annually in the United States

definition: A rotator cuff tear (ICD-10: M75.1 for rotator cuff tear; M75.10 unspecified; M75.11 for right, M75.12 for left) is a full-thickness or partial-thickness disruption of the tendinous insertion of one or more rotator cuff muscles onto the humerus. Full-thickness tears span the entire tendon width from articular (joint) surface to bursal (outer) surface; partial-thickness tears involve only one surface. The rotator cuff functions as a dynamic stabilizer of the glenohumeral joint — the supraspinatus initiates arm abduction, the infraspinatus and teres minor externally rotate the arm, and the subscapularis internally rotates the arm — while the tendons form a contiguous cuff around the humeral head to maintain centering within the glenoid socket during movement. Tears compromise this stabilizing function, leading to superior migration of the humeral head, altered glenohumeral kinematics, pain, and weakness. Tears are classified by size (small <1 cm, medium 1-3 cm, large 3-5 cm, massive >5 cm), location (articular-sided, bursal-sided, interstitial), acuity (acute vs. chronic/degenerative), and reparability (repairable vs. irrepairable due to tendon retraction, muscle atrophy, and fatty degeneration).

typesClassification.description: A tear involving only a portion of the tendon thickness — either on the articular (joint) side (more common, accounting for 60% of partial tears) or the bursal (outer) side. Articular-sided tears are often managed with debridement or repair depending on depth (>50% of tendon thickness warrants repair).

A tear that spans the entire width of the tendon from articular to bursal surface, creating a hole or gap. The tendon retracts from its insertion site in proportion to tear chronicity. May be small and focal or large and U-shaped involving multiple tendons.

A tear caused by a discrete injury event — a fall on an outstretched arm (FOOSH injury), direct blow to the shoulder, sudden forceful pull (e.g., catching a heavy object), or dislocation. More common in younger patients. Often involves a previously healthy tendon and is typically more reparable. Urgent surgical repair is often recommended for active patients with large acute tears.

A tear resulting from years of progressive tendinopathy, microtearing, and structural failure of the tendon, often without a clear single traumatic event. Associated with age-related changes in tendon vascularity, collagen quality, and repetitive overhead activity. More common in the dominant arm. Tears tend to be chronic with tendon retraction and fatty muscle degeneration, making repair more challenging.

A tear involving two or more tendons and/or measuring greater than 5 cm in anterior-posterior dimension. A distinct clinical entity because these tears are frequently irrepairable due to tendon retraction, severe muscle atrophy, and fatty degeneration (Goutallier Grade 3 or 4). Treatment options include: repair when possible, superior capsule reconstruction, tendon transfers, reverse shoulder arthroplasty in older patients, or conservative management.

primarySymptoms: Shoulder pain, typically localized to the anterolateral aspect of the shoulder and upper arm — worsened by overhead activities, reaching behind the back, and lifting objects away from the body.

Weakness in shoulder abduction, external rotation, or internal rotation depending on which tendon(s) are torn — patients often notice difficulty lifting the arm overhead, washing hair, or reaching into a cabinet.
Pain at night, particularly when lying on the affected shoulder or rolling onto the unaffected shoulder — a hallmark of rotator cuff pathology that disrupts sleep in the majority of patients.

Limited active range of motion — the patient cannot lift the arm fully despite having adequate strength when the examiner lifts it for them (a distinction from frozen shoulder where both active and passive motion are limited).
Crepitus or a grinding sensation with shoulder movement — felt when the rough tear edges move against each other or against the acromion.
A sense of instability or 'catching' in the shoulder joint with certain movements.

Atrophy of the supraspinatus or infraspinatus muscle — visible as a hollowing above or below the scapular spine in large chronic tears (indicates long-standing, massive tear with poor prognosis for repair).
earlyWarningSigns: New shoulder pain after a fall, heavy lift, or sports injury — particularly if the arm was in an overhead position
Weakness when lifting the arm overhead that was not present before — difficulty with activities like reaching overhead, washing hair, or putting items on high shelves
Shoulder pain that worsens at night when lying on that side
Pain or weakness when performing previously easy tasks — opening a jar, lifting a moderate weight, or performing overhead motions at the gym
A 'pop' or tearing sensation in the shoulder followed by immediate pain and weakness — suggests an acute tear requiring urgent evaluation
Gradual onset of shoulder pain and weakness in a dominant arm over months, particularly in someone over 50
emergencySigns: Sudden, severe shoulder weakness or inability to lift the arm following trauma — may indicate a large acute tear requiring urgent surgical evaluation
Shoulder pain, weakness, and numbness after a shoulder dislocation — the axillary or suprascapular nerve may be injured
Fever, chills, and severe shoulder pain with marked limitation of motion — may indicate septic arthritis of the shoulder joint, a surgical emergency
Severe pain and inability to use the arm after a fall, especially in an older patient — a proximal humerus fracture or severe rotator cuff tear should be ruled out
Chest pain radiating to the left shoulder — can indicate cardiac event and requires immediate cardiac evaluation

causes.description: A discrete injury event causing immediate structural failure of the rotator cuff tendon. Common mechanisms: fall on an outstretched arm (FOOSH), direct blow to the shoulder, forceful traction (grabbing a rail during a fall), forced external rotation, or shoulder dislocation. Acute tears occur most commonly in patients aged 40-60 who sustain a significant injury.

The most common cause of rotator cuff tears overall — years of repetitive overhead activities (tennis, painting, manual labor, swimming) cause progressive tendon fiber deterioration that eventually exceeds the tendon repair capacity. The supraspinatus tendon has a hypovascular zone approximately 1 cm from its insertion, making it particularly vulnerable to degenerative changes with aging.

Tendon quality deteriorates with advancing age due to decreased fibroblast cellularity, reduced collagen cross-linking, decreased glycosaminoglycan content, and reduced vascularity. MRI studies of asymptomatic individuals show tear prevalence of 5% in those under 30, 15% in ages 50-59, 25% in ages 60-69, and over 50% in those over 70.

causes.cause: Subacromial impingement and extrinsic tendon compression
causes.description: Narrowing of the subacromial space (from acromial spurring, hook-type acromion, or osteophytes) compresses the supraspinatus tendon between the humeral head and acromion during arm elevation. Chronic extrinsic compression causes tendon fraying, microtearing, and eventual tearing — the 'impingement syndrome' model of rotator cuff disease.

Biological changes within the tendon itself — not just mechanical factors — drive tear development. Intrinsic factors include: cellular senescence, oxidative stress accumulation, advanced glycation end products (AGEs) in collagen, and reduced tenocyte synthetic activity. These changes are accelerated by smoking, diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, and corticosteroid use.

Smoking causes significant vasoconstriction, reducing blood flow to the rotator cuff tendons. Nicotine impairs fibroblast proliferation and collagen synthesis. Smokers have a 3-4 fold increased risk of rotator cuff tears and significantly worse outcomes after surgical repair.

Repetitive shoulder dislocations or chronic instability (common in swimmers, overhead athletes) can cause attritional rotator cuff tears as the tendons are stressed with abnormal humeral head translation.

riskFactors.description: The single strongest risk factor for rotator cuff tears. Degenerative changes accumulate in tendon tissue with age, making tears increasingly common.
Occupations and sports requiring repetitive overhead arm use — painters, carpenters, construction workers, swimmers, tennis players, baseball players — are at significantly elevated risk.

Smoking causes vasoconstriction and impairs tendon healing at the cellular level. Smokers have a 3-4 fold increased risk of rotator cuff tears and significantly higher failure rates after surgical repair.
Diabetes impairs collagen cross-linking, reduces tendon healing capacity, and is associated with 2-3 fold increased risk of rotator cuff tears.
Previous shoulder surgery, dislocation, or fracture alters anatomy and scar tissue, increasing the risk of subsequent rotator cuff pathology.
A hooked (Type III) or downward-sloping (Type II) acromion narrows the subacromial space and causes extrinsic compression of the supraspinatus tendon.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of rotator cuff disease
riskFactors.description: There is a genetic component to rotator cuff tears — a family history of rotator cuff pathology increases individual risk.
Elevated LDL cholesterol is associated with increased rotator cuff tear risk, likely through deposition of lipids in tendon tissue causing structural compromise.

affectedDemographics: Rotator cuff tears are the most common cause of shoulder pain and disability in adults over 40; symptomatic tears affect approximately 5-10% of the general adult population
Asymptomatic tears are far more common — MRI studies show 13% of people in their 50s, 20% in their 60s, and over 50% of people over 70 have rotator cuff tears without knowing it
Men are affected more frequently than women in working-age populations; the gender difference narrows in older age groups
Dominant arm tears occur in approximately 90% of unilateral cases
Athletes in overhead sports — baseball pitchers (50-70% have tears), swimmers (50-80%), tennis players — have dramatically elevated rates of rotator cuff pathology
Workers in manual labor trades — construction, painting, electrical work — have occupational rotator cuff tear rates 2-5 times higher than sedentary workers
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of a rotator cuff tear begins with a clinical history focusing on the onset of pain and weakness (traumatic vs. gradual), aggravating activities, night pain, and functional limitations. Physical examination tests the rotator cuff systematically: the 'drop arm test' assesses supraspinatus function; the 'external rotation lag sign' assesses infraspinatus/teres minor; the 'lift-off test' assesses subscapularis. Palpation identifies tenderness over the specific tendon. The painful arc test (pain with active abduction between 60-120 degrees) suggests impingement often associated with tears. Imaging: X-rays (AP, scapular Y, axillary lateral) are obtained first to rule out arthritis, fractures, and to assess acromial morphology. MRI is the gold standard for confirming and characterizing rotator cuff tears — it accurately determines tear size, tendon retraction, muscle atrophy, fatty degeneration, and surgical reparability.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A battery of targeted clinical tests provides strong evidence for or against a rotator cuff tear. Key tests: Drop Arm Test (supraspinatus), External Rotation Lag Sign (infraspinatus/teres minor), Lift-off Test and Belly-press Test (subscapularis), and Neer and Hawkins impingement signs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner will ask you to move your arm in specific directions, resist downward pressure at different arm positions, and hold certain positions against gravity.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain radiographs (X-rays) — AP, scapular Y, axillary lateral
diagnosticTests.purpose: X-rays do not show tendons but reveal bony pathology that may mimic or contribute to rotator cuff tears: acromial morphology, acromial spurs, osteoarthritis of the AC joint, humeral head position (superior migration suggests large tear), and proximal humerus fractures.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard X-ray — three views of the shoulder. Takes 5-10 minutes, minimal radiation exposure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: MRI accurately depicts the rotator cuff tendons, confirming presence, location, size, and pattern (partial vs. full-thickness) of tears. Crucially, it also assesses tendon retraction, muscle atrophy, fatty degeneration (Goutallier grade), and integrity of the biceps tendon and labrum — all essential for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie inside the MRI scanner with your arm in a specific position. The shoulder protocol takes 30-45 minutes. You must remain still. The machine is loud; earplugs are provided.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Dynamic, real-time imaging of the rotator cuff. Can demonstrate tendon discontinuity, tendon thinning, fluid in the subacromial bursa, and can assess tendon integrity during movement. Less expensive than MRI and without radiation. Operator-dependent.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld probe is moved over the shoulder with acoustic gel. You may be asked to move your arm during the exam. Takes 15-20 minutes. Results are immediate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: MRI performed after intra-articular injection of gadolinium contrast dye. Provides superior visualization of partial-thickness tears (particularly articular-sided tears), labral tears, and biceps tendon pathology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A radiologist injects contrast dye into the shoulder joint under fluoroscopy (X-ray guidance) before the MRI. The injection may cause brief discomfort.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of rotator cuff tears is individualized based on tear characteristics (size, acuity, chronicity), patient age and activity level, functional demands, and presence of muscle atrophy or fatty degeneration. Acute traumatic tears in active patients are generally treated surgically to restore tendon length and prevent retraction and muscle degeneration. Degenerative or chronic tears in older, low-demand patients are initially managed conservatively. First-line conservative treatment includes: activity modification avoiding aggravating overhead activities, physical therapy focusing on rotator cuff strengthening, scapular stabilization, and progressive range of motion; NSAIDs for pain control; subacromial corticosteroid injection for pain relief; and patience. Surgical options include: arthroscopic repair (most common, using suture anchors to re-attach the tendon to bone), open repair (for large or complex tears), and tendon transfer (for massive irreparable tears). Post-surgical rehabilitation requires 6-12 weeks of protected passive motion followed by progressive strengthening over 4-6 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Physical therapy and structured rehabilitation
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of non-operative management and essential for post-surgical recovery. Phase 1 (0-4 weeks): passive range of motion, pendulum exercises, isometric strengthening. Phase 2 (4-12 weeks): active-assisted then active range of motion, scapular stabilization. Phase 3 (3-6 months): progressive rotator cuff and deltoid strengthening with resistance bands and weights.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-operative treatment is successful in 70-80% of patients with degenerative, low-demand tears. Surgical repair achieves good-to-excellent outcomes in 85-95% of small and medium tears.

medicalTreatments.name: Subacromial corticosteroid injection
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of triamcinolone or methylprednisolone into the subacromial bursa reduces inflammation and pain, allowing more effective participation in physical therapy. Provides pain relief within 3-7 days lasting 4-12 weeks. Multiple injections (>3) risk tendon softening and possible acceleration of tearing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides short-to-medium term pain relief in 60-75% of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (oral and topical)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral NSAIDs reduce pain and inflammation in the acute phase. Topical diclofenac gel applied to the shoulder has evidence of efficacy with minimal systemic side effects. Used as part of multimodal treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 60-70% of patients report meaningful short-term pain relief.

medicalTreatments.name: Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) injection
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of concentrated growth factors from the patient's own blood into the tear site to stimulate tendon healing. Evidence for efficacy is mixed — some RCTs show improved healing rates in small-to-medium tears, others show no significant benefit over placebo.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mixed evidence — not yet standard of care. Some studies show modest benefit in small tears.

medicalTreatments.name: Biceps tenotomy or tenodesis
medicalTreatments.description: For associated biceps tendinopathy (which frequently coexists with rotator cuff tears). The biceps tendon is either cut (tenotomy) or cut and reattached to the humerus (tenodesis). Reduces shoulder pain originating from the biceps tendon.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for treating associated biceps pathology, present in 20-30% of rotator cuff tear patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic rotator cuff repair
surgicalOptions.description: The most common surgical approach. Small incisions (portals) are made around the shoulder, and an arthroscope (camera) and instruments are introduced. The tear edges are debrided, the bone surface is prepared, and suture anchors are placed in the humeral head. The torn tendon is advanced and secured to the bone using the sutures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rates for structural healing: 90-95% for small tears, 80-90% for medium tears, 70-80% for large tears, and 50-60% for massive tears.

surgicalOptions.name: Open or mini-open rotator cuff repair
surgicalOptions.description: A single larger incision (open) or a smaller incision with arthroscopic assistance (mini-open) exposes the tear directly. Allows for more robust tendon-to-bone repair in large or complex tears, particularly those with significant retraction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable to arthroscopic repair for large and massive tears when performed by experienced surgeons.

surgicalOptions.name: Superior Capsule Reconstruction (SCR)
surgicalOptions.description: A newer procedure for massive, irreparable rotator cuff tears. A dermal allograft or autograft is placed between the glenoid and greater tuberosity to reconstruct the superior capsule, functioning as a 'biological spacer' that depresses the humeral head and restores glenohumeral stability.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Early-to-mid term studies show good pain relief and improved function in 70-85% of patients with massive irreparable tears.

surgicalOptions.name: Reverse Total Shoulder Arthroplasty (RTSA)
surgicalOptions.description: For elderly patients (>65-70) with massive, irreparable rotator cuff tears and associated glenohumeral arthritis ('cuff tear arthropathy'), a reverse shoulder replacement is the preferred reconstruction. The prosthesis flips the ball-and-socket geometry, allowing the deltoid muscle to compensate for the non-functional rotator cuff.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Excellent pain relief (90%+), good to excellent functional improvement in 80-90% of properly selected patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Tendon transfers — latissimus dorsi or pectoralis major transfer
surgicalOptions.description: Used for massive, irreparable tears in younger patients (<60-65) who wish to avoid joint replacement. A functional tendon is transferred to the greater tuberosity to restore external or internal rotation function.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable — 60-80% good-to-excellent outcomes depending on patient selection, tear pattern, and surgical technique.

alternativeTreatments.name: Structured physical therapy — the primary evidence-based non-operative approach
alternativeTreatments.description: A supervised physical therapy program with a shoulder-specialized therapist, progressing through passive, active-assisted, active, and resisted phases over 6-12 months. Emphasis on scapular stabilization (trapezius, serratus anterior), rotator cuff strengthening (external rotation with band, prone Y-T-W exercises), and functional movement retraining.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: 70-80% success rate for non-operative management of degenerative tears in low-demand patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Traditional Chinese acupuncture or Western medical acupuncture targeting shoulder musculature and local tender points. May provide modest short-term pain relief through release of endogenous opioids and improved local blood flow. Used as an adjunct to physical therapy.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Low-quality evidence for modest short-term pain relief. Not recommended as a standalone treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Extracorporeal Shockwave Therapy (ESWT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Acoustic pressure waves applied to the shoulder to stimulate healing in chronic rotator cuff tendinopathy. Evidence for rotator cuff tears specifically is limited.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Limited evidence for efficacy in rotator cuff tears specifically. More established for calcific tendinitis.

whySeeSpecialist: An orthopedic surgeon specializing in shoulder surgery should be seen: following an acute traumatic shoulder injury with significant pain and weakness; when conservative management has failed after 6-12 weeks; when symptoms are severe enough to prevent work or sleep; when there is visible muscle atrophy in the shoulder; when a massive tear is suspected; or when considering surgical repair.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an orthopedic surgeon who has completed a shoulder and elbow fellowship or has a specific focus on rotator cuff surgery. Ask about their annual volume of rotator cuff repairs (higher volume correlates with better outcomes). For non-operative management, a physical therapist with orthopedic specialization (OCS — Orthopedic Clinical Specialist certification) is the most important clinician. Continuity is essential.
preventionStrategies: Regular rotator cuff and scapular stabilization strengthening — particularly for overhead athletes and manual workers; the 'thrower's ten' exercise program is well-established
Avoiding repetitive overhead strain without adequate rest and recovery
Maintaining good shoulder mobility — avoiding shoulder stiffness (which increases subacromial impingement risk) through regular gentle stretching
Smoking cessation — smoking dramatically accelerates rotator cuff tendon degeneration
Managing diabetes and hypercholesterolemia — both conditions impair tendon health and healing
Using proper lifting technique — avoiding heavy overhead lifts without adequate shoulder strength and core stability
lifestyleModifications: Sleep with the affected arm elevated on a pillow or in a recliner during the acute painful phase — lying directly on the shoulder worsens night pain
Avoid overhead reaching, heavy lifting, and carrying objects away from the body during the acute phase
Apply ice to the shoulder for 15-20 minutes several times daily during acute flares, particularly after physical therapy
Use a shoulder sling briefly (1-3 days) after an acute tear, then discontinue to prevent stiffness — prolonged immobilization is counterproductive
Adapt your workspace — ergonomic adjustments (raising the keyboard, using a headset) reduce shoulder strain during computer work
Maintain general upper body fitness with exercises that do not aggravate the shoulder — stationary biking, walking, and lower body strength training

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet — Mediterranean-style diet rich in omega-3 fatty fish, olive oil, vegetables, and nuts reduces systemic inflammation that impairs tendon healing
Collagen-supporting nutrients — adequate vitamin C (citrus, peppers, leafy greens), proline (bone broth, egg whites), and lysine (legumes, dairy) support tendon repair and collagen synthesis
Protein adequacy — sufficient protein intake (1.2-1.6 g/kg body weight in those recovering from injury) supports tissue healing
Vitamin D and calcium — adequate intake supports musculoskeletal health; vitamin D deficiency is associated with tendinopathy
Water — well-hydrated connective tissue has better mechanical properties and healing capacity
avoid: Smoking — the single most important modifiable risk factor for rotator cuff tears and impaired healing
Excessive alcohol — alcohol impairs protein synthesis and tissue healing
Processed foods and refined sugars — these promote systemic inflammation that impairs tendon healing
Excessive corticosteroid use (oral or injectable) — corticosteroids impair tenocyte function and may weaken tendons with repeated use

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise therapy for rotator cuff tears progresses through defined phases under the guidance of a physical therapist: Phase 1 (Weeks 0-4 post-injury or post-surgery): passive range of motion only (pendulum exercises), no active use of the rotator cuff. Phase 2 (Weeks 4-8): active-assisted range of motion, scapular stabilization exercises (prone Y-T-W at 30 degrees), isometrics. Phase 3 (Weeks 8-12): active range of motion, progressive strengthening with resistance bands (external rotation, internal rotation, row), scapular endurance. Phase 4 (Months 3-6): advanced strengthening, functional training, sport-specific or occupation-specific exercises. Return to full activity is typically permitted at 6-9 months post-surgery for small tears and 9-12 months for large and massive tears.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed home exercises daily — consistency is the primary determinant of non-operative and post-surgical outcomes
Use ice after physical therapy sessions and when pain flares — 15-20 minutes, several times daily during the acute phase
Sleep with the arm supported on a pillow (sleeping in a recliner is often more comfortable initially)
Avoid the provocative positions that cause pain — typically overhead activities, reaching behind the back, and carrying heavy objects at arm's length
Use the unaffected arm for daily tasks initially, gradually reintegrating the affected arm as strength returns
Track your progress with a simple log — range of motion measurements and strength milestones
prognosis: Prognosis for rotator cuff tears depends on multiple factors. Tear size is the strongest predictor — small tears (<1 cm) have 90-95% healing rates and excellent functional outcomes; medium tears (1-3 cm) 80-90%; large tears (3-5 cm) 70-80%; massive tears (>5 cm) 50-60%. Patient age is critical — patients over 65-70 have lower healing rates and less dramatic functional improvement from repair. Muscle atrophy and fatty degeneration (Goutallier Grade 3+) are the strongest negative predictors — once muscle has converted to fat, it does not recover even with a technically successful repair. Fatty degeneration is irreversible. Patient compliance with post-operative rehabilitation is essential.
recoveryTimeline: Non-operative treatment: initial pain improvement within 2-4 weeks of starting physical therapy; meaningful functional improvement at 3-6 months; maximum improvement at 6-12 months. Surgical repair: immobilization in a sling for 6 weeks; passive range of motion begins at 6 weeks post-op; active-assisted motion at 8-10 weeks; progressive strengthening at 12-16 weeks; light functional activities at 4-6 months; full return to heavy labor or overhead sport at 9-12 months. Re-tear rates are highest in the first 6 months post-op. Full remodeling of the repair site takes 12-18 months.
complications: Post-traumatic adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) — particularly after prolonged immobilization following acute tears; characterized by progressive loss of both active and passive range of motion
Failed repair (re-tear) — the most common complication after surgical repair, occurring in 20-40% of large and massive tears even with appropriate rehabilitation
Chronic pain and weakness — particularly in massive tears where the rotator cuff cannot be fully restored; may require salvage procedures (reverse shoulder arthroplasty) years later
Deltoid deconditioning — from prolonged immobilization in a sling; addressed through specific deltoid strengthening exercises
Neurovascular injury — rare but serious complication of shoulder surgery; the axillary, musculocutaneous, or suprascapular nerve may be injured during repair
Stiffness and loss of motion — particularly after open repair or prolonged immobilization; in severe cases, manipulation under anesthesia may be needed
Wound complications — infection, dehiscence, or hypertrophic scarring after surgical repair, more common in open repairs

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Rotator Cuff Injuries
supportResources.description: AAOS patient education resource on rotator cuff tears — causes, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Professional society for shoulder surgeons providing patient education materials on rotator cuff tears.

supportResources.name: NICE NG101: Shoulder Injury Guidelines. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2020.
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline for the assessment and management of shoulder injuries including rotator cuff tears.

supportResources.name: Hughes J, Tokish JM. Rotator Cuff Tears. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024.
supportResources.description: Comprehensive clinical overview of rotator cuff tear epidemiology, anatomy, classification, and management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Adhesive Capsulitis (Frozen Shoulder)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Frozen shoulder causes global restriction of both active AND passive range of motion — you cannot lift your arm even with help. Rotator cuff tears limit active motion but passive motion is preserved — the examiner can lift your arm fully.

confusedWithConditions.name: Subacromial Impingement Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Impingement syndrome is pain caused by compression of the rotator cuff tendons under the acromion, often associated with bursitis. It may or may not involve an actual tendon tear.

confusedWithConditions.name: Calcific Tendinitis of the Shoulder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Calcific tendinitis involves deposition of calcium hydroxyapatite crystals within a rotator cuff tendon, causing severe inflammation and pain. Visible on X-ray as calcific deposits in the tendon.

confusedWithConditions.name: Biceps Tendinitis / Biceps Tenosynovitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Biceps tendinitis causes anterior shoulder pain at the front of the shoulder and in the bicipital groove, often with a 'pop' in the front of the shoulder with movement. May coexist with rotator cuff tears but can occur independently.

relatedConditions.name: Subacromial Impingement Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Impingement syndrome frequently precedes and often coexists with rotator cuff tears — extrinsic compression of the supraspinatus tendon under the acromion causes progressive tendon damage that may culminate in tearing.

relatedConditions.name: Adhesive Capsulitis (Frozen Shoulder)
relatedConditions.relevance: Frozen shoulder frequently develops after rotator cuff injury or surgery, particularly with prolonged immobilization.

relatedConditions.name: Calcific Tendinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Calcific tendinitis is thought by some to be a precursor to rotator cuff tearing — calcium deposits in the tendon may cause local inflammatory reactions that weaken the tendon structure.

relatedConditions.name: Acromioclavicular Joint Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: AC joint osteoarthritis causes pain at the top of the shoulder. It frequently coexists with rotator cuff tears in older patients.

faqs.question: How serious is a rotator cuff tear?
faqs.answer: The seriousness of a rotator cuff tear depends on its size, acuity, and the patient's functional demands. Small tears in older, low-activity patients are managed effectively without surgery and have an excellent prognosis. Large or massive tears in young, active patients are more serious — they have lower healing rates after repair, higher re-tear rates, and can progress to irreversible muscle degeneration (fatty atrophy) that permanently compromises shoulder function. Acute traumatic tears in active patients are generally considered serious because delay in repair leads to tendon retraction and muscle degeneration that worsens outcomes.

faqs.question: Can a rotator cuff tear heal on its own?
faqs.answer: A full-thickness rotator cuff tear will not heal on its own — the tendon has retracted away from the bone and the gap fills with scar tissue rather than tendon. However, non-operative management (physical therapy) can produce excellent functional outcomes because strengthening the remaining rotator cuff and other shoulder muscles compensates for the lost tendon function. Studies of non-operative treatment show that 70-80% of patients achieve meaningful pain relief and functional improvement despite the tear persisting on imaging.

faqs.question: When is rotator cuff surgery necessary?
faqs.answer: Surgery is generally recommended when: (1) An acute traumatic tear in an active patient of any age — delay in repair leads to tendon retraction and muscle degeneration that worsens outcomes. (2) A large (3-5 cm) or massive (>5 cm) tear — these have progressively worsening prognosis with non-operative care and benefit from repair when anatomically feasible. (3) Failure of 6-12 months of conservative treatment in a patient whose symptoms significantly impair quality of life, work, or sleep. (4) A massive irreparble tear in an elderly patient — may benefit from reverse shoulder arthroplasty.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from rotator cuff surgery?
faqs.answer: Recovery from rotator cuff repair is a long, staged process: Weeks 1-6: sling immobilization, no active use of the arm; pendulum exercises and passive flexion only. Weeks 6-12: sling discontinued, passive range of motion restored, active-assisted motion begins. Weeks 12-16: active range of motion, light strengthening begins. Months 4-6: progressive strengthening, return to light daily activities. Months 6-9: return to most normal activities, light labor. Months 9-12: full return to heavy labor and overhead sport. Complete biological healing and remodeling of the tendon-to-bone repair takes 12-18 months.

faqs.question: What is the recovery time without surgery?
faqs.answer: Non-operative recovery from a rotator cuff tear is faster in the early phase because there is no surgical recovery, but the functional outcome may not be as complete as surgical repair for large tears. Initial pain improvement typically occurs within 2-4 weeks of starting physical therapy. Meaningful functional improvement is usually seen at 3-6 months. Maximum non-operative benefit is typically achieved at 6-12 months. The tear itself persists on imaging, but the shoulder often functions well because the remaining intact rotator cuff and peri-scapular muscles compensate.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with a torn rotator cuff?
faqs.answer: Exercise is both the primary treatment for non-operative management and the essential rehabilitation component after surgical repair — but specific exercises must be appropriate for the tear severity and phase of healing. During the acute painful phase, only pain-free, gentle range of motion exercises are permitted. Heavy overhead lifting, push-ups, and pulling exercises are avoided until the rotator cuff has healed. Physical therapy provides a structured, progressive exercise program that gradually loads the tendon in a controlled manner.

faqs.question: What does a rotator cuff tear feel like?
faqs.answer: The most common symptom is shoulder pain — often described as aching or sharp — localized to the front and outer aspect of the shoulder and upper arm. The pain typically worsens with overhead activities (reaching overhead, washing hair, putting items on high shelves) and when lying on the affected shoulder at night. Weakness is the other hallmark symptom — you may notice difficulty lifting your arm, particularly for overhead activities. Some patients report a clicking, catching, or grinding sensation in the shoulder. A large acute tear may cause immediate, severe weakness and an inability to lift the arm.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for a rotator cuff tear?
faqs.answer: The best treatment is individualized based on tear severity, patient age, activity level, and functional goals. For small (<1 cm), non-traumatic tears in patients over 60 with low physical demands: physical therapy is the best treatment. For acute traumatic tears of any size in active patients under 65: surgical repair is generally recommended. For medium tears (1-3 cm): the decision is individualized — physical therapy trial first is reasonable in low-demand patients; repair is preferred in younger active patients. For large and massive tears: repair when anatomically feasible, considering tendon transfer or reverse shoulder arthroplasty for irreparrable tears.

faqs.question: Will a rotator cuff tear get worse without surgery?
faqs.answer: Without surgery, the tear may enlarge over time — studies show that 40-50% of degenerative rotator cuff tears enlarge within 1-2 years. However, this does not always translate to worsening symptoms. Many patients maintain function despite tear progression because the remaining intact rotator cuff and peri-scapular muscles compensate effectively. The tear is more likely to enlarge if: the patient is young and active; the tear is large to begin with; there is significant fatty atrophy already present; and activities continue to stress the tendon.

faqs.question: How is a rotator cuff tear diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with a clinical evaluation — your doctor will ask about the onset of pain and weakness, aggravating activities, night pain, and any recent injury. They will examine your shoulder, testing specific movements and applying resistance at different angles to isolate each rotator cuff muscle. Specific tests like the drop arm test, external rotation lag sign, and lift-off test help identify which tendon(s) are torn. X-rays are obtained to assess the bones and acromial shape. MRI is the definitive test — it shows the exact size, location, and pattern of the tear, the degree of tendon retraction, and the condition of the rotator cuff muscles.

faqs.question: Is rotator cuff surgery risky?
faqs.answer: Rotator cuff repair is generally a safe and effective surgery, but it carries real risks. Complications occur in approximately 5-10% of cases and include: infection (1-2%), nerve injury (particularly axillary or suprascapular nerve), stiffness and adhesive capsulitis (most common complication), re-tear (20-40% for large/massive tears), deltoid deconditioning, and anesthesia-related risks. The decision to proceed with surgery weighs the risks of surgery against the natural history of the tear and the patient's functional impairment.

faqs.question: What is a massive rotator cuff tear?
faqs.answer: A massive rotator cuff tear is defined as a tear involving two or more tendons and/or measuring greater than 5 cm in its anterior-posterior dimension. These are clinically distinct from smaller tears because: they are frequently irreparable — tendon retraction and fatty muscle degeneration may make it impossible to bring the tendon back to its anatomical footprint; they cause significant weakness and dysfunction that is difficult to compensate for; and they have a high rate of tear progression. Treatment options for massive tears include: repair when possible, superior capsule reconstruction, tendon transfers, reverse total shoulder arthroplasty (in older patients), and conservative management.

metaTitle: Rotator Cuff Tear: Symptoms, Causes, Treatment & Surgery | aihealz
metaDescription: Rotator cuff tear causes shoulder pain and weakness. Learn about symptoms, diagnosis, surgery options, recovery time, and physical therapy. Reviewed by our medical team.

images.altText: Diagram of the shoulder showing the four muscles and tendons of the rotator cuff: supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis
images.caption: The rotator cuff consists of four tendons — supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis — that stabilize the shoulder joint.

images.altText: Detailed anatomy of the shoulder joint showing the rotator cuff tendons and surrounding structures
images.caption: The rotator cuff tendons form a continuous cuff around the humeral head, maintaining its centering within the glenoid socket during arm movement.

images.altText: Illustration of arthroscopic rotator cuff repair technique showing suture anchors placed in the humeral head
images.caption: Arthroscopic rotator cuff repair uses suture anchors to re-attach the torn tendon to the humeral bone.
